title: BreakfastFlow: Managing Hotel Breakfast Demand
shortDescription: A QR-based booking system that helps hotel guests avoid crowded breakfast hours using real-time availability and flexible booking.
overview: Based on my experience working in gastronomy and hotel environments, I observed how peak hours often become overcrowded, leading to long wait times, overwhelmed staff, and a poor guest experience.
introduction: Based on my experience working in gastronomy and hotel environments, I observed how peak hours often become overcrowded, leading to long wait times, overwhelmed staff, and a poor guest experience.
problemStatement: Guests often arrive at hotel breakfast at the same time due to a lack of visibility into occupancy, resulting in overcrowding, long waits, and frustration. At the same time, staff lack tools to manage demand and distribute guest flow effectively.
keyInsight: Redistributes demand across time slots, reduces peak-time congestion, and improves both guest experience and operational efficiency.

processNarrative.description: Help guests choose less crowded breakfast times to reduce congestion, while giving staff clear insight into demand and keeping the experience flexible.

I designed a QR-based booking system that reduces breakfast congestion by guiding guests toward less crowded time slots through real-time occupancy insights. Guests can explore availability, see peak times, and optionally reserve a slot while retaining the flexibility to arrive without booking.

To support decision-making, the system uses clear visual states ({{green:quiet}}, {{orange:steady}}, {{red:full}}) and automatically validates capacity to prevent overcrowding.

**Core concept:** Influence behavior without enforcing it.

Guests access the system via QR code, view breakfast availability, and select a preferred time slot. The system validates availability in real time and suggests alternatives if a slot is unavailable. After booking, guests can manage their reservation by modifying or canceling it as needed.

Time slots that are in the past or already full are shown but cannot be selected. Slot availability is checked in real time before a booking is confirmed. When a selected slot is no longer available, the system recommends another option. Guests can choose between reserving in advance or arriving more flexibly

learnings: Translated a real-world service problem into a clear and structured digital solution.|Shifted from screen-level thinking to system-level design, focusing on flows, rules, and interactions.|Used simple visual signals to improve clarity and support user decision-making.|Designed for both user needs and operational constraints, balancing flexibility with control.|Reduced complexity by prioritizing essential features and keeping the experience intuitive.
metrics: **Visible availability states** make occupancy easy to understand
**Disabled unavailable slots** reduce mistakes while preserving clarity
**Alternative suggestions** keep users moving forward instead of interrupting the flow
**A lightweight booking flow** makes the experience quick and easy to use
**Flexible booking behavior** balances guest choice with operational needs

title: Reducing Scheduling Conflicts in Hospitality Through Real-Time Shift Management
shortDescription: Coordinating staff schedules while allowing flexible shift swaps with manager approval.
overview: While working in hospitality, I observed that shift coordination relied on manual communication, making it difficult to manage changes, preferences, and last-minute updates.
introduction: While working in gastronomy and hotel environments, I experienced firsthand how shift coordination often relies on manual communication, making it difficult to manage swaps, preferences, and last-minute changes.
problemStatement:
Shift management is often handled through static schedules or informal channels (e.g. WhatsApp, printed rosters), leading to:

• Lack of real-time visibility

• High coordination effort for managers

• Frequent miscommunication

This leads to understaffed shifts, increased workload for managers, and frustration for staff.
keyInsight: Most issues don’t come from the schedule itself, but from last-minute changes and the lack of real-time updates.
designGoal: I prioritised real-time updates and automated validation over static scheduling to reduce coordination overhead and prevent operational failures.
hypothesis:
Designed a mobile-first system enabling staff to manage shifts and request swaps in real time, while managers maintain reliable staffing through automated validation and approval workflows.

This approach reduces manual coordination and improves reliability in high-pressure hospitality environments.
tradeoffs: A fully flexible system would increase user freedom but risk understaffing and service disruption.
processIntro: Structured shift swap and validation logic through user flows, then tested interaction patterns using low- and mid-fidelity wireframes.
learnings: Designed for multiple user roles, balancing staff flexibility with managerial control.|Simplified complex operational rules (e.g. shift validation) into clear, intuitive interactions.|Learned that most scheduling issues stem from real-time changes, not initial planning.
metrics: Prioritised simplicity over flexibility to reduce coordination effort in high-pressure environments
Introduced rule-based validation to prevent understaffing and ensure reliable shift coverage
Designed role-based workflows to balance staff flexibility with managerial control
Used clear system feedback to help users understand outcomes and reduce confusion
